summary: Reporter finds SVG output from SKSvgCanvas with SKManagedWStream is missing the closing </svg> tag because MemoryStream.ToArray() is called before the canvas is disposed — the SVG canvas writes the closing tag during its native destructor, so the stream must be read after canvas disposal.

stepsToReproduce: Create a MemoryStream and wrap it in SKManagedWStream
Create an SKSvgCanvas using SKSvgCanvas.Create(bounds, stream)
Call managedStream.ToArray() to capture SVG bytes while canvas is still alive (using var declaration)
Observe that the SVG output is missing the closing </svg> tag
reproEvidence.environmentDetails: SkiaSharp 3.119.0, Blazor + iOS via Rider, reproduced in release builds on all browsers

screenshots.description: SVG rendering correctly after manually adding closing tag

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The SVG canvas has always written the closing tag during destructor — the reporter likely had a different code structure (scoped using block) in 2.88 era that disposed the canvas before reading the stream.

regression.reason: The SVG canvas has always written the closing </svg> tag during native canvas destruction. The SkiaSharp test SvgCanvasSavesFile uses a scoped using block that correctly disposes the canvas before reading the stream. The reporter's use of 'using var' (C# 8 declaration syntax) means disposal happens at end of scope, after ToArray(). This is a code pattern change by the reporter, not a SkiaSharp regression.

analysis.summary: The SVG canvas writes the closing </svg> tag when its native destructor runs (via sk_canvas_destroy during Dispose). The reporter uses 'using var' declarations which delay disposal to end-of-scope, but calls MemoryStream.ToArray() before that point. The existing test SvgCanvasSavesFile demonstrates the correct pattern: scoped using block, then read stream after disposal.
analysis.rationale: Classified as type/bug because the reporter describes broken output. However, the behavior is by-design — SVG closing tag is written during canvas destruction, which is the expected Skia behavior. The reporter's code reads the stream prematurely. The claim of regression from 2.88.9 is likely due to a code pattern change (switching from scoped using blocks to using var declarations). Recommending close-as-by-design with a workaround showing the correct disposal pattern.

keySignals.text: it certainly used to work, but it could well have been broken for a while (maybe since 3.X release?)
keySignals.interpretation: Uncertain regression claim. The SVG lifecycle hasn't changed — more likely the reporter changed their code pattern when upgrading.

keySignals.text: Looking into it, it might be SKManagedWStream specific. SKFileWStream seems to output a correct SVG with close tags.
keySignals.interpretation: SKFileWStream 'works' because the file is read after all disposals complete. With MemoryStream, the reporter reads it before canvas disposal.

keySignals.text: using var managedStream = new MemoryStream(); using var stream = new SKManagedWStream(managedStream); using var canvas = SKSvgCanvas.Create(bounds, stream);
keySignals.interpretation: All three using var declarations defer disposal to end of enclosing scope. ToArray() is called before that point.

codeInvestigation.finding: SKSvgCanvas.Create(bounds, SKWStream) uses SKObject.Referenced — canvas references but doesn't own the stream. The closing </svg> is written by Skia's ~SkCanvas destructor which calls sk_canvas_destroy.
DisposeNative calls sk_canvas_destroy, which triggers the SVG canvas destructor that writes the closing </svg> tag and flushes to the stream.
SvgCanvasSavesFile test uses a scoped using block: the canvas is disposed first, then the stream is read. This is the correct pattern and it successfully parses the complete SVG.
The Stream overload of Create() internally creates an SKManagedWStream and owns it via SKObject.Owned. This overload would also require canvas disposal before reading the underlying stream.
OnFlush delegates to stream.Flush(). The flush is triggered by Skia during canvas destruction, confirming data is only fully written after disposal.

workarounds: Use a scoped using block for the canvas so it disposes before reading the stream: using (var canvas = SKSvgCanvas.Create(bounds, stream)) { /* draw */ } then call managedStream.ToArray()
Alternatively, pass the MemoryStream directly to SKSvgCanvas.Create(bounds, memoryStream) which uses the Stream overload, and read the stream after the scoped using block ends
nextQuestions: Could a Flush method on the canvas or a dedicated EndDocument-style API improve discoverability of this pattern?

resolution.hypothesis: The reporter reads the MemoryStream before the SVG canvas is disposed. The closing </svg> tag is written during canvas destruction (the standard Skia behavior). Using 'using var' instead of scoped 'using' blocks causes the disposal order to not match the data access pattern.

proposals.title: Use scoped using block for canvas
proposals.description: Replace 'using var canvas = ...' with a scoped 'using (var canvas = ...) { }' block, and move the stream read after the block ends. This ensures the canvas writes the closing </svg> tag before the stream is read.

proposals.title: Pass MemoryStream directly
proposals.description: Use the Stream overload of SKSvgCanvas.Create instead of wrapping in SKManagedWStream. Same disposal pattern applies — canvas must be disposed before reading.

resolution.recommendedProposal: Use scoped using block for canvas
resolution.recommendedReason: Minimal code change — just restructure the using statements. Matches the existing test pattern in SvgCanvasSavesFile.

actionability.reason: The SVG canvas writes the closing tag during disposal — this is standard Skia behavior. The reporter's code reads the stream before disposal. The existing test demonstrates the correct pattern.

actions.description: Apply bug label and area label
Explain the disposal ordering requirement with a workaround code snippet
Close as by-design — disposal ordering is working correctly
